perturbation.original_text: 10. Remedies . The Receiving Party shall indemnify and hold the Disclosing Party harmless from any damages, losses, costs, or lia bilities arising out of or resulting from any unauthorized use or disclosure by the Receiving Pa rty or its Representatives of the Confidential Information or the Confidential Ma terials or other breach of this Agreement by the Receiving Party or its Representatives, including, but not limited t o, reasonable attorneys’ fees, court costs and other litigation expenses incurred in connection with the enforcem ent of this Agreement. Given the nature of the Confidential Information and the Confidential Materials and the damage that would result to the Disclosing Party if informati on contained therein were disclosed to any third party, money damages would not be a sufficient remedy for any breach of this Agreement by the Receiving Party or its Representatives. Therefore, in addition to all other remedies, the Disclosing Party shall be entitled to specifi c performance and injunctive or ot her equitable relief as a remedy for any such breach or threatened breach, and th e Receiving Party and its Representatives waive any requirement for the securing or posting of any bond in connection with such remedy.
perturbation.changed_text: 10. Remedies. The Receiving Party shall only be liable for direct, proven damages, losses, or liabilities that demonstrably arise out of the Receiving Party’s intentional unauthorized use or disclosure of Confidential Information. Attorney's fees and costs are capped at $5,000. If Confidential Information is disclosed inadvertently despite reasonable precautions, the Receiving Party's sole obligation will be to notify the Disclosing Party and make reasonable efforts to recover the information. Specific performance and injunctive relief will not be available to the Disclosing Party, with monetary damages being the exclusive remedy.
perturbation.explanation: The change significantly limits the Receiving Party's liability by requiring intentional unauthorized use and capping attorney's fees. This conflicts with the original text and creates **in-text contradiction** regarding remedies. The original provision allowed for specific performance and injunctive relief, while the modified version excludes these remedies entirely, limiting the Disclosing Party to monetary damages only.
perturbation.contradicted_text: Remedies . The Receiving Party shall indemnify and hold the Disclosing Party harmless from any damages, losses, costs, or lia bilities arising out of or resulting from any unauthorized use or disclosure by the Receiving Pa rty or its Representatives of the Confidential Information or the Confidential Ma terials or other breach of this Agreement by the Receiving Party or its Representatives, including, but not limited t o, reasonable attorneys’ fees, court costs and other litigation expenses incurred in connection with the enforcem ent of this Agreement. Given the nature of the Confidential Information and the Confidential Materials and the damage that would result to the Disclosing Party if informati on contained therein were disclosed to any third party, money damages would not be a sufficient remedy for any breach of this Agreement by the Receiving Party or its Representatives. Therefore, in addition to all other remedies, the Disclosing Party shall be entitled to specifi c performance and injunctive or ot her equitable relief as a remedy for any such breach or threatened breach, and th e Receiving Party and its Representatives waive any requirement for the securing or posting of any bond in connection with such remedy.
perturbation.justification: The changed text significantly alters the remedies available to the Disclosing Party in case of a breach. The original text and contradicted text state that specific performance and injunctive relief are available, and monetary damages may not be sufficient. However, the changed text explicitly states that these remedies are not available and limits the Disclosing Party to monetary damages. This creates a direct and strong contradiction regarding the remedies, making it an in-text contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: 14. Notice and Service of Process. All service of process, not ices, consents, requests, claims, demands, instructions or other communicati ons to be given hereunder by the Parties shall be in writing. All such serv ice of process, notices, consen ts, requests, claims, demands, instructions or other communicati ons may be given personally, by re gistered or certified mail (with proof of receipt, postage and expe nses prepaid, return receipt requested), express package service, facsimile transmission, or electronic mail transmi ssion. All such notices shall be deemed to be received as follows: (i) if delivered personally, wh en received, (ii) if mailed, three (3) days after being mailed, (iii) if sent by express package servic e, when signed for, (iv) if sent by facsimile transmission, when the fax has transmitted over the telephone lines, as evidenced by a fax confirmation report generated by the transmitting mach ine (provided that a copy of such notice is also sent via U.S. mail, postage prepaid), and (v) if sent by electronic mail transmission, two (2) days after being sent. All notices shall be sent to the Parties at their respective addresses set forth on Exhibit B attached to this Agreement. Th e Receiving Party acknowledges and agrees that all (a) requests for Confidential Information or Conf idential Materials, (b) communications regarding the Possible Transaction, (c) requests for facility tours or meetings, and (d) discussions or questions regarding procedures, shall be submitted or directed to Soneet Kapila of the Disclosing Party, unless otherwise directed in writing by Soneet Kapila.
perturbation.changed_text: 14. Notice and Service of Process. All notices and communications must be exclusively delivered via certified mail with return receipt requested. Service of process shall be governed by the Florida Rules of Civil Procedure. Electronic mail and facsimile transmissions are explicitly disallowed for formal notifications under this agreement. For routine inquiries regarding the Possible Transaction or requests for Confidential Information, the Receiving Party may contact the general inquiries email address provided on the Disclosing Party's website.
perturbation.explanation: This alteration mandates certified mail as the sole acceptable method for notices and communications, explicitly prohibiting electronic mail and facsimile, which directly **contradicts** the original provision allowing multiple methods. This will creates contradiction with Exhibit B in the file where email and fax details are mentioned, and if the user uses it for communication then he/she might breach the contract.
perturbation.contradicted_text: EXHIBIT B FOR THE RECEIVING PARTY: Telephone No: Facsimile No.: Email: Notices sent to the Receiving Party under th is Agreement to be sent with a copy to: Telephone No: Facsimile No.: Email: FOR THE DISCLOSING PARTY: Soneet Kapila, Assignee KapilaMukamal, LLP 1000 South Federal Highway, Suite 200 Fort Lauderdale, Florida 33316 Telephone No: 954/712-3201 Facsimile No.: 954/761-1033 Email: skapila@kapilamukamal.com Notices sent to the Disclosing Party under th is Agreement to be sent with a copy to: Edward J. Peterson, Esq. Matthew B. Hale, Esq. Stichter, Riedel, Blain & Postler, P.A. 110 East Madison Street, Suite 200 Tampa, Florida 33602 Telephone No.: 813/229-0144 Facsimile No.: 813/229-1811 Email: epeterson@srbp.com ; mhale@srbp.com
perturbation.justification: The changed text explicitly mandates certified mail as the exclusive method for formal notifications, disallowing electronic mail and facsimile. This directly contradicts Exhibit B, which provides facsimile and email addresses for both the Receiving Party and the Disclosing Party. The original text allowed for multiple forms of communication. By limiting it to only certified mail, the change directly contradicts the existing contact information provided in Exhibit B, creating a strong in-text contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: 17. Term . Unless otherwise set forth in this Agreement, this Agreement shall terminate on the earlier to occur of: (i) the consummation of a Possi ble Transaction pursuant to a 8 Definitive Agreement, and (ii) two (2) years fr om the date that the Parties terminate their discussions and consideration of a Possible Transaction; provide d, however, that all Confidential Information and Confidential Material s shall continue to be subject to the terms of this Agreement after the termination of this Agreement.
perturbation.changed_text: 17. Term. This Agreement shall terminate upon the earlier of (i) the execution of a Definitive Agreement concerning the Possible Transaction, or (ii) one (1) year from the Effective Date of this Agreement. Following termination, there shall be no further obligations of either party, except that the Receiving Party shall safeguard any remaining tangible Confidential Information for a period of 90 days, after which all such Confidential Information shall be securely destroyed and certification of destruction provided to the Disclosing Party. The survival clause related to confidentiality of information no longer applies, except trade secrets.
perturbation.explanation: The change shortens the term of the agreement from two years to one year and introduces a 90-day period for safeguarding tangible confidential information after termination, after which destruction is required. It also removes the survival clause for non-trade secret confidential information. This presents an **in-text contradiction** by conflicting with Section 2, which states that trade secrets enjoy perpetual protection, and Section 6, which implies that confidentiality continues indefinitely, creating confusion on the actual period of protection of the data.
perturbation.contradicted_text: 2. Trade Secrets . All trade secrets of the Assignors also shall be entitled to all of the protections and benefits of the Florida Uniform Trade Secrets Act (the “Trade Secrets Act”) and any other applicable laws. If any information that the Disclosing Party deems to be a trade secret of the Assignors is found by a court of competent ju risdiction not to be a tr ade secret, then such information nevertheless shall be considered Confidential Information for purposes of this Agreement. In the case of trade secrets, the Receiving Party waives any requirement that the Disclosing Party submit proof of the economic valu e of any trade secret or post a bond or other security. Additionally, the R eceiving Party acknowledges that nothing in this Agreement (including Section 17 below) shall place a time lim itation on the period of protection of such trade secret, it being agreed that th e trade secrets of the Assignors shall enjoy perpetual protection against unauthorized disclosure or use, subject onl y to the provisions of the Trade Secrets Act and other applicable laws.
perturbation.justification: The changed text shortens the agreement's term and modifies the confidentiality obligations, explicitly stating that the survival clause for non-trade secret confidential information no longer applies, with a 90 day limit. This directly contradicts Section 2, which states that trade secrets enjoy perpetual protection and implies indefinite confidentiality through out the agreement, thus creating a strong in-text contradiction based on the definition of 'Ambiguities - In Text Contradiction'.

perturbation.original_text: 21. Publicity . No publicity, release or announcem ent concerning this Agreement or the transactions contemplated hereby shall be made by either Party without advance written approval thereof by the other Part y. The Receiving Party will not use or permit the use of the Disclosing Party’s or any Assi gnor’s name, logos, trademarks or other identifying data, or otherwise discuss or make reference to the Disclo sing Party or the Assignors, in any notices to third parties, any promotional or marketing mate rial or in any press release or other public announcement or advertisement, however charac terized, without the Di sclosing Party’s prior written consent.
perturbation.changed_text: 21. Publicity. Neither party shall make any announcement regarding this agreement without prior written consent of the other, which consent shall not be unreasonably withheld. However, the Receiving Party is authorized to disclose the existence of this Agreement and the general nature of the Possible Transaction to its legal counsel, financial advisors, and potential financing sources without obtaining prior approval, provided that they are bound by similar confidentiality obligations.
perturbation.explanation: This change introduces an exception to the publicity restriction, allowing the Receiving Party to disclose the agreement and the Possible Transaction to certain advisors without prior approval. This **contradicts** the initial blanket prohibition on publicity. In the original paragraph it needs approval from the other party but here is not the case. It does creates contradiction here.
perturbation.contradicted_text: Publicity . No publicity, release or announcem ent concerning this Agreement or the transactions contemplated hereby shall be made by either Party without advance written approval thereof by the other Part y. The Receiving Party will not use or permit the use of the Disclosing Party’s or any Assi gnor’s name, logos, trademarks or other identifying data, or otherwise discuss or make reference to the Disclo sing Party or the Assignors, in any notices to third parties, any promotional or marketing mate rial or in any press release or other public announcement or advertisement, however charac terized, without the Di sclosing Party’s prior written consent.
perturbation.justification: The changed text modifies the original publicity clause by introducing an exception, permitting the Receiving Party to disclose the agreement to specific advisors without prior approval. This directly contradicts the original, which mandates advance written approval from the other party for any announcement concerning the agreement. This creates an in-text contradiction within the document, because the first section states that any announcement needs approval, whereas the modified section allows for disclosure to certain individuals without this approval. This makes it a strong contradiction because it creates uncertainty about the publicity restrictions.
